inline_review:
commit 284fdcfb90ab6ebefe3f62a4e9c85b35ca8fc51a
Author: Valentin Schneider <vschneid@redhat.com>

x86/mm, mm/vmalloc: Defer kernel TLB flush IPIs when tracking CR3 switches

This commit adds a mechanism to defer kernel TLB flush IPIs for NOHZ_FULL
CPUs executing in userspace. It relies on the CR3 switch that occurs when
transitioning back to kernel mode to perform the necessary TLB flush.

>     Combined with:
>       o the CR3 switch itself being a flush for non-global mappings
>       o global mappings under kPTI being limited to the CEA and entry text
>     we now have a way to safely defer (kernel) TLB flush IPIs targeting

Is the assumption about the CR3 switch always flushing non-global mappings
accurate for modern systems?

When Page Table Isolation (KPTI) and PCID are enabled, the kernel typically
uses the CR3_NOFLUSH bit (bit 63) during the switch to kernel space to avoid
flushing the kernel PCID's TLB entries for performance.

If flush_tlb_kernel_range_deferrable skips the IPI, and the hardware
actively avoids the flush during the CR3 switch by setting CR3_NOFLUSH,
will the target CPU return to kernel mode retaining stale TLB entries?
Could this lead to a use-after-free when the freed vmalloc physical
pages are reallocated?

> diff --git a/arch/x86/mm/tlb.c b/arch/x86/mm/tlb.c
> index f5b93e01e347..e08f16474f07 100644
> --- a/arch/x86/mm/tlb.c
> +++ b/arch/x86/mm/tlb.c

[ ... ]

> @@ -1556,13 +1558,31 @@ void flush_tlb_kernel_range(unsigned long start, unsigned long end)
>  
> +void flush_tlb_kernel_range(unsigned long start, unsigned long end)
> +{
> +	__flush_tlb_kernel_range(NULL, start, end);
> +}
> +
> +#ifdef CONFIG_TRACK_CR3
> +static bool flush_tlb_kernel_cond(int cpu, void *info)
> +{
> +	return housekeeping_cpu(cpu, HK_TYPE_KERNEL_NOISE) ||
> +	       per_cpu(kernel_cr3_loaded, cpu);
> +}

Can a race condition occur between the hardware CR3 switch and the software
flag update due to Store-Load reordering?

If a CPU entering kernel space updates kernel_cr3_loaded, that store
might be delayed in the store buffer. Meanwhile, speculative or
architectural kernel memory accesses could execute and cache TLB entries.

Concurrently, a CPU unmapping a page and clearing the PTE might read
kernel_cr3_loaded as false and skip the IPI.

Would this sequence result in the entering CPU retaining a stale TLB entry?

Additionally, if an NMI fires during the narrow window between the flag
update and the actual CR3 switch, the NMI handler would execute in kernel
mode while the CPU is marked as being in userspace. Would this cause the
CPU to miss deferred IPIs entirely?
output:
{"findings":[{"problem":"Deferred kernel TLB flushes cause NOHZ_FULL CPUs in userspace to retain stale kernel PCID TLB entries, leading to Use-After-Free of vmalloc physical pages.","severity":"Critical","severity_explanation":"The safety of deferring the kernel TLB flush IPI relies on the assumption stated in the commit message: 'the CR3 switch itself being a flush for non-global mappings' when the CPU transitions back to kernel space. However, when Page Table Isolation (KPTI) and PCID are enabled (which is the standard configuration on modern x86 CPUs), `SWITCH_TO_KERNEL_CR3` explicitly sets the `CR3_NOFLUSH` bit (bit 63) in the CR3 register to avoid flushing the kernel PCID's TLB entries for performance. Because `flush_tlb_kernel_range_deferrable()` skips the IPI, and the hardware actively avoids the flush during the CR3 switch, the target CPU returns to kernel mode with stale TLB entries indefinitely. When the freed virtual addresses are reallocated, the CPU will silently access the previously freed physical pages, resulting in a deterministic cross-subsystem Use-After-Free and severe memory corruption."},{"problem":"Race conditions between the hardware CR3 switch and the `kernel_cr3_loaded` software flag allow stale TLB entries to be cached via Store-Load reordering.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"Even if PCID were disabled and the CR3 switch did flush the TLB, the x86 memory model allows Store-Load reordering, which creates a classic Dekker's algorithm race. When a CPU enters kernel space, it updates `kernel_cr3_loaded` and switches CR3. If `kernel_cr3_loaded` is updated after the CR3 switch, the store can be delayed in the store buffer while subsequent speculative or architectural kernel memory accesses execute and cache TLB entries. Concurrently, a CPU unmapping a page clears the PTE (using a full barrier via `xchg`) and reads `kernel_cr3_loaded` as false, thus skipping the IPI. This results in the entering CPU retaining a stale TLB entry. Similarly, if an NMI fires during the narrow window between the flag update and the CR3 switch, the NMI handler executes in kernel mode while the CPU is marked as being in userspace, missing IPIs entirely."}],"fixes":"","review_inline":"commit 284fdcfb90ab6ebefe3f62a4e9c85b35ca8fc51a
